title: Movement, Mobility & Physical Literacy
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance

required.title: [Equipment] A pull-up bar or sturdy overhead bar

suggested.title: [Video] YouTube: MovNat or Shirley Darlington-Rowley movement progressions
[Equipment] Gymnastic rings (hung from a tree or beam)

supplementary.title: [Equipment] Balance beam or 2x4 on the ground

competence_markers.description: The student can bear crawl, crab walk, and army crawl for 20+ yards each with control and coordination
The student can climb a rope, tree, or wall at least 6 feet off the ground with confidence (not necessarily speed)
The student can broad jump their own height in distance and land quietly with stable knees
The student can perform a forward roll, backward roll, and side fall from standing with proper form — chin tucked, head protected, impact distributed
The student can balance-walk across a 2x4 or low beam for 10+ feet forward and backward without stepping off
The student can dead hang from a bar for 30+ seconds (60 seconds is the full target)
The student can carry an object equal to 25% of their bodyweight for 100+ yards without setting it down
The student demonstrates comfort and confidence in natural terrain — moves across uneven ground, through obstacles, and off the ground without hesitation or excessive caution
The student throws and catches a ball with reasonable accuracy using both hands
The student sprints with natural mechanics — midfoot landing, arm drive, forward lean — without prompting

cross_connections.description: Daily hanging, weekly movement circuits, and consistent outdoor time are habit disciplines. The student who does these without being reminded is demonstrating IC1.1 competence in a physical context.
Climbing higher than is comfortable, falling practice, and carrying heavy loads all involve managing discomfort and fear. These are IC3 exercises in physical form.
The movement vocabulary built here is the foundation for the strength and conditioning work in 2.1.2. A child who moves well is ready to get strong. A child who does not move well is not.
Falling safely transfers directly to martial arts. The forward roll and breakfall in this packet are the same techniques used in BJJ and wrestling when you are taken down.
Outdoor movement skills transfer directly to fieldwork. Carrying materials, climbing to gather resources, navigating terrain — these are survival skills built through movement training.
Balance, coordination, and body awareness built here are the physical substrate for dance. The child who moves well can learn to dance well.

content.why_this_matters:
Your body is the most important tool you will ever own. It is the only one you cannot replace.

Right now, while you are young, your body is learning faster than it ever will again. Every time you climb a tree, jump over a ditch, roll down a hill, balance on a log, or hang from a bar, your brain is wiring itself to control your body better. This is happening right now, at this age, faster and more powerfully than it will at any other time in your life. If you spend these years sitting on a couch or staring at a screen, that window does not wait for you. It closes.

The good news is that what you need to do is what you already want to do — move. Run. Climb. Jump. Roll. Crawl. Hang. Carry things. Explore. The difference between this packet and "just playing" is that you are going to do it with purpose. You are going to learn specific skills. You are going to practice things that are hard until they become easy. And you are going to build a body that can do things most people cannot — not because you are special, but because you trained and they did not.

A person who can move well can do almost anything. They can climb to safety. They can carry someone who is hurt. They can fall without getting injured. They can work all day without breaking down. They can play any sport, learn any skill, and handle any physical challenge that life puts in front of them. That is what you are building.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **A broad vocabulary of human movement** — crawling, climbing, jumping, landing, rolling, balancing, hanging, carrying, throwing, catching, sprinting, and moving across natural terrain
2. **The ability to fall safely** — one of the most practically important physical skills a person can have
3. **Comfort with being off the ground** — climbing, hanging, and swinging without fear
4. **Balance and coordination** — static and dynamic, on stable and unstable surfaces
5. **Grip strength and hanging endurance** — the foundation of upper body capability
6. **Confidence in physical challenge** — the knowledge that difficult physical tasks can be broken into steps and mastered through practice
7. **The habit of daily physical movement** — not as exercise class but as a natural part of life
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Movement Vocabulary — What Your Body Can Do

Human beings are designed to move in many different ways. Not just running. Not just walking. Your body can crawl, climb, jump, land, roll, balance, hang, swing, carry, throw, catch, sprint, and move across every kind of terrain on earth. These are not "exercises." They are the movements your body was built for — the same movements that kept human beings alive for hundreds of thousands of years before anyone invented a gym.

Most people today use almost none of these movements. They walk on flat surfaces, sit in chairs, and occasionally jog on a treadmill. Their bodies can do extraordinary things, but they never ask them to. Over time, the body loses what it does not use. Joints stiffen. Muscles weaken. Balance disappears. By the time most adults are 40, they cannot squat to the ground, hang from a bar for 30 seconds, or roll without hurting themselves.

You are going to be different. You are going to build every one of these movement patterns now, while your body learns them fastest, and you are going to keep them for life.

### Part 2: Crawling — The Foundation You Think You Outgrew

Crawling is not just for babies. It is one of the most complete movement patterns the human body can perform. Different crawling patterns train coordination, core strength, shoulder stability, hip mobility, and the ability to move your arms and legs in opposition — a skill called contralateral movement that is fundamental to how your nervous system organizes motion.

**Crawling patterns you will practice:**

- **Bear crawl** — hands and feet on the ground, hips up, moving forward. Keep your knees off the ground and your hips level. This is harder than it looks. Do it well before you do it fast.
- **Crab walk** — belly up, hands and feet on the ground, moving backward or sideways. Trains the back of your body — shoulders, triceps, glutes — which most people neglect.
- **Army crawl** — belly on the ground, pulling yourself forward with your elbows and pushing with your toes. Low and slow. This is how you move under obstacles.
- **Quadrupedal movement** — any crawling pattern done over varied surfaces: grass, dirt, rocks, sand, slight inclines. The uneven surface forces your body to constantly adjust, which builds balance and coordination that flat-ground crawling cannot.

**Practice:** Crawl for distance and time. Bear crawl 50 yards. Crab walk across the yard. Army crawl under a low obstacle. Vary the surface. Vary the speed. Do this regularly until crawling feels as natural as walking.

### Part 3: Climbing — Getting Off the Ground

Climbing is one of the most natural human movements. Children climb instinctively — trees, rocks, furniture, fences, anything with a handhold. This instinct is correct. Climbing builds grip strength, pulling strength, body awareness, problem-solving, and confidence in a way that no other movement can match.

**What you will climb:**

- **Trees** — the original climbing structure. Start low. Learn to read a tree: which branches are strong enough to hold you, which are dead (they snap), where to place your feet. Never climb higher than you are willing to fall from until your skills are solid.
- **Ropes** — a thick rope hung from a tree branch or beam. Start by just hanging. Then learn to climb using your feet to grip (the J-hook or S-wrap technique). Rope climbing is one of the best grip strength builders that exists.
- **Rocks and boulders** — natural rock faces with holds and crevices. Start on low rocks (bouldering height — no higher than you can safely jump down from). Learn to read the rock: where are the handholds? where are the footholds? What is your plan before you start?
- **Walls and structures** — playground walls, climbing walls, fences (with permission). Vertical and horizontal traversing — moving sideways across a surface is different from moving up, and trains different skills.

**The key skill in climbing:** Planning. Before you grab the first hold, look at the whole route. Where are you going? What comes after the first move? Good climbers think ahead. They do not just grab and pull.

### Part 4: Jumping and Landing — Force Production and Force Absorption

Jumping is how your body produces force against the ground. Landing is how your body absorbs force from the ground. Both matter. Most people can jump passably. Almost nobody lands well. Bad landings are how people destroy their knees, ankles, and spines. Good landing mechanics — learned now — protect you for life.

**Jumping skills:**

- **Broad jump** — jump forward for distance from two feet. Swing your arms, drive your hips forward, and land on two feet.
- **Vertical jump** — jump straight up for height. Drive through the balls of your feet.
- **Single-leg hops** — hop forward on one foot. This exposes any imbalance between your left and right sides.
- **Precision jumps** — jump to a specific target (a rock, a line, a spot on the ground) and stick the landing. This is the foundation of parkour and it trains your brain to control exactly where your body goes.

**Landing mechanics — the critical skill:**

- Land on the balls of your feet, not your heels
- Bend your knees as you land — your legs are shock absorbers
- Keep your knees tracking over your toes — do not let them collapse inward
- Absorb the force progressively — soft and quiet, not stiff and loud
- The rule: if your landing is loud, it is wrong. A good landing is quiet because the force is being absorbed through the full range of your joints, not slammed into the ground.

**Practice:** Jump off progressively higher surfaces (start at 12 inches, progress slowly) and focus entirely on landing quietly with good mechanics. A child who can drop from 3 feet and land in silence with stable knees has better landing mechanics than most adult athletes.

### Part 5: Falling Safely — The Skill That Prevents Injury

This is one of the most important physical skills in this entire pillar. You will fall thousands of times in your life — off bikes, on ice, during sports, tripping over obstacles, during martial arts training. The difference between a fall that results in a broken wrist and a fall that you roll through and stand up from is technique. This technique can be learned, and now is the time to learn it.

**Falling skills:**

- **Forward roll** — tuck your chin, round your back, roll diagonally from one shoulder to the opposite hip. Your head never touches the ground. Practice on grass first, then harder surfaces. This is your primary "bail out" — if you are going forward and cannot stop, you roll.
- **Backward roll** — tuck your chin to your chest, sit back, roll over your rounded back, land on your feet. Your head never touches the ground. The chin-to-chest position is the key — it protects your neck.
- **Side fall (breakfall)** — when you are going to your side, slap the ground with your arm and leg simultaneously to distribute the impact across a wide area instead of a single point. The arm hits the ground at about 45 degrees from your body. Practice this from a kneeling position first, then standing.
- **Back fall** — tuck your chin, round your back, slap both arms against the ground as you land. The arm slap absorbs energy that would otherwise go into your spine.

**The principle:** Distribute impact across the largest possible area and keep your head off the ground. A fall onto a single point (an outstretched hand, a knee, an elbow) concentrates force and causes fractures. A fall distributed across a rolling surface disperses force and causes bruises at worst.

**Practice:** Every training session includes falling practice. Roll forward, backward, and to each side. Practice from standing. Practice from a light jog. Practice on grass, dirt, and mats. You are building reflexes — when your body starts to fall, the roll should be automatic, not something you have to think about.

### Part 6: Balancing — Owning Your Center of Gravity

Balance is the ability to control your center of gravity over your base of support. That sounds technical. It means: can you stand on a narrow surface without falling off? Can you walk across a log? Can you stand on one foot with your eyes closed?

Balance is trained, not given. A child who practices balance regularly will have better balance at age 8 than an adult who never practices it. And balance matters everywhere — on ladders, on rocks, on ice, on boats, on bicycles, on skis, and in every sport that exists.

**Balance progressions:**

- **Stand on one foot** — 30 seconds per side. When that is easy, close your eyes. When that is easy, stand on an unstable surface (a pillow, a foam pad, soft ground).
- **Walk a line** — tape on the floor or a line on the sidewalk. Heel-to-toe. Then do it on a 2x4 laid on the ground. Then do it on a beam or rail at low height.
- **Balance beam walks** — forward, backward, sideways. On a low beam (2x4 on the ground). Progress to a beam at 6 inches, 12 inches, 18 inches as confidence grows.
- **Natural terrain balance** — walk across rocks in a stream. Walk along a fallen log. Navigate a rocky hillside. This is the real test — surfaces that are uneven, unpredictable, and different every time.

**The key:** Practice balance every day. Stand on one foot while brushing your teeth. Walk the curb instead of the sidewalk. Balance is a skill that improves rapidly with daily practice and deteriorates rapidly without it.

### Part 7: Hanging and Swinging — Grip Strength and Shoulder Health

Hanging from a bar is the most important thing you can do for your shoulders and your grip strength. It decompresses your spine, opens your shoulders, and builds the grip endurance that every other upper-body skill depends on.

**Hanging progressions:**

- **Dead hang** — grab the bar, hang with straight arms, feet off the ground. Hold as long as you can. Time it. Your goal: hang for 60 seconds. Most adults cannot do this.
- **Active hang** — from the dead hang position, pull your shoulders down and back (away from your ears) without bending your elbows. This engages the muscles that stabilize your shoulder joint. Alternate between dead hang and active hang.
- **Swinging** — swing forward and back on the bar. Control the swing — do not just flail. This builds dynamic shoulder stability.
- **Traversing** — move hand-over-hand along a bar, monkey bars, or a horizontal rope. This is brachiating — the movement pattern that primates use to move through trees. It is extraordinarily good for shoulder health and grip strength.

**The habit:** Hang from your pull-up bar every day. When you pass it, grab it and hang. Make it a habit like brushing your teeth. Grip strength is the foundation of upper body capability, and daily hanging is the simplest way to build it.

### Part 8: Carrying — The Most Useful Strength Skill

The ability to pick something up and carry it somewhere is the most transferable physical skill there is. Every real-world physical task involves carrying: groceries, firewood, tools, water, building materials, an injured person. If you can carry heavy things for distance, you are useful. If you cannot, you are a bystander.

**Carrying variations:**

- **Farmer's walk** — carry a heavy object in each hand (buckets, sandbags, dumbbells, water jugs) and walk. Keep your chest up, shoulders back, core tight. Start with short distances and build.
- **Bear-hug carry** — wrap your arms around a heavy, awkward object (sandbag, stone, bag of mulch) and carry it. This trains your whole body.
- **Overhead carry** — carry something heavy overhead and walk. This demands core stability and shoulder control. Start light.
- **Partner carry** — carry a training partner (piggyback, fireman's carry). This is both strength training and a practical survival skill — the ability to carry someone who cannot walk.

**Practice:** Help carry things. Volunteer to carry the groceries, the firewood, the camping gear. Carry rocks. Carry sandbags. The more you carry, the stronger you get, and the more useful you become.

### Part 9: Running, Sprinting, and Changing Direction

Running is the most basic human locomotion. Sprinting — running as fast as you can — is a different skill from jogging. Both matter. Changing direction while running — cutting, pivoting, dodging — matters even more, because real-world running almost never happens in a straight line.

**Running skills:**

- **Natural gait running** — run with a slight forward lean, land on the midfoot or forefoot, keep your cadence quick (short, fast steps rather than long, slow strides). Do not heel-strike — it is a brake, not a propulsion.
- **Sprinting** — all-out effort for 20–60 yards. Sprint mechanics are different from distance running: higher knee drive, more aggressive arm swing, more forward lean. Sprint regularly — it builds explosive power, speed, and anaerobic conditioning.
- **Terrain running** — run on trails, grass, dirt, hills, and uneven ground. This forces your body to adapt to changing surfaces and builds ankle stability, proprioception, and agility that flat-surface running cannot produce.
- **Direction change** — tag, chase games, dodging drills. Plant and cut. Pivot and go. The ability to change direction at speed is one of the most useful athletic skills and one of the most fun to practice.

### Part 10: Throwing and Catching

Throwing and catching are fundamental coordination skills that train hand-eye coordination, timing, spatial awareness, and bilateral development (using both sides of your body).

**Throwing practice:**

- Overhand throws — with rocks, balls, sticks, bean bags. Both hands. Your non-dominant hand needs practice too.
- Underhand tosses — for accuracy at short range.
- Sidearm throws — skipping rocks is the classic sidearm throw, and it is legitimate throwing practice.

**Catching practice:**

- Start with large, soft objects (beach balls, bean bags) and progress to smaller, harder objects (tennis balls, baseballs).
- Practice catching with two hands, then one hand, then while moving.

The goal is not to become a quarterback. It is to develop the hand-eye coordination and bilateral control that throwing and catching uniquely build.

### Part 11: Outdoor Unstructured Movement — The Original Training Environment

The most important movement training for a child your age is not a program or a class. It is time spent outside, on natural terrain, moving freely. Playing in woods, fields, streams, rocks, and mud. Climbing trees. Jumping over ditches. Balancing on logs. Building forts. Chasing friends. Exploring.

This is not recess. It is the training environment that human bodies evolved in. Every surface is different. Every step requires adjustment. You cannot predict what comes next, so your body learns to adapt constantly. A child who spends hours outside on varied terrain develops balance, coordination, proprioception, and physical confidence that no structured program can match.

**The commitment:** Spend time outside every day. Not on a sports field (though that counts too). In natural terrain where the ground is uneven, the surfaces are unpredictable, and you have to figure out how to move through it. This is the foundation beneath everything else in this packet.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Crawling Patterns
**Developing well:** The student moves fluidly in bear crawl, crab walk, and army crawl. Their opposite arm and leg move together (contralateral pattern). They can sustain crawling for 20+ yards without stopping or collapsing.

**Developing slowly:** Movement is jerky and uncoordinated. Same-side arm and leg move together (ipsilateral pattern — normal in very young children but should resolve by age 5-6). They fatigue quickly or refuse to crawl because "it's for babies."

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student getting enough crawling practice? Have you framed it as a skill challenge rather than a baby activity? Bear crawl races and obstacle courses make crawling competitive and fun.

### 2. Climbing Confidence
**Developing well:** The student climbs trees, ropes, walls, and rocks with confidence. They look for handholds and plan their route before starting. They can climb at least 6 feet off the ground without panic.

**Developing slowly:** The student is fearful of leaving the ground. They climb 2-3 feet and freeze. They refuse to try new climbing surfaces.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student had sufficient exposure to climbing? Fear of heights is natural and resolves with gradual exposure, not with forced climbing. Start low — very low — and build confidence one foot at a time over weeks. If the student has had a bad fall, they may need extra time at low heights before progressing.

### 3. Landing Mechanics
**Developing well:** Jumps from low heights (12-24 inches) and lands quietly on the balls of the feet with bent knees. Knees track over toes. No collapsing, no loud impact. Can stick the landing and hold balance.

**Developing slowly:** Lands flat-footed or on heels. Knees collapse inward (valgus). Landing is loud and jarring. Cannot maintain balance on landing.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you cueing "land quiet"? The auditory feedback of a quiet landing is the simplest and most effective teaching tool. If the student can hear the difference between a loud landing and a quiet one, they can self-correct.

### 4. Falling Safely
**Developing well:** Forward roll, backward roll, and side fall are smooth and controlled from standing. Chin is tucked every time. Head never contacts the ground. The student rolls instinctively when they trip or lose balance during play.

**Developing slowly:** Rolls are stiff and awkward. The student puts their hands out to catch themselves instead of rolling. Chin tuck is inconsistent. The student avoids falling practice.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is falling practice happening weekly? This skill requires repetition over months, not a single session. Are you practicing on appropriate surfaces? Has the student seen you roll? Demonstration matters — if you can do a forward roll, do one.

### 5. Balance
**Developing well:** Walks a 2x4 beam forward and backward without stepping off. Stands on one foot for 15+ seconds per side. Navigates uneven natural terrain with confidence.

**Developing slowly:** Falls off the beam immediately. Cannot stand on one foot for more than a few seconds. Avoids uneven terrain or moves across it extremely slowly with visible anxiety.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is there a balance surface accessible for daily practice? Balance improves rapidly with daily exposure — even 2 minutes of beam walking per day produces measurable improvement within weeks.

### 6. Hanging Endurance
**Developing well:** Dead hang for 30+ seconds. Attempts active hangs and traversing. Hangs from the bar daily without being reminded.

**Developing slowly:** Cannot hang for more than 10 seconds. Avoids the bar. Grip gives out immediately.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the bar accessible? Is the student hanging daily? Grip strength develops quickly in children with daily practice. If the bar is too thick for small hands, wrap it with tape or use a thinner bar. If the student has never hung before, 10 seconds is a reasonable starting point — build from there.
